title: Mediating a Roommate Dispute
summary: A resident advisor mediates a conversation with a tenant whose relationship with his flatmate has broken down over noise, guests, and unpaid bills.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Thanks for coming in, Marcus. I've already spoken to Jordan separately, and I wanted to hear your side before we sit down with both of you together.

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Thanks for setting this up. I should say upfront — I don't want to make Jordan look bad. I just want the situation to be liveable again.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: That's a good place to start. Could you tell me, in your own words, what's been going wrong over the last month?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Three things, really. The noise, the guests, and the bills. Each one on its own I could probably handle. All three at once is wearing me down.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Let's take them one at a time. Tell me about the noise.

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Jordan plays music until two or three most weeknights, and I have classes at eight. I've asked twice politely. The first time he turned it down for a night. The second time, he said I was being uptight. After that I stopped asking.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: I can understand why that would feel discouraging. Did you ever set agreed quiet hours when you moved in?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: No, that's part of the problem. We didn't really set any rules. I assumed it would be common sense, but our common sense isn't the same.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: That's very common. Setting expectations after the fact is harder than setting them at the start. Let's come back to that. Tell me about the guests.

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: His partner stays four or five nights a week. I never agreed to that, and the apartment is small. The bathroom is constantly busy, and I've started showering at the gym just to get a normal morning.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Have you raised this with him?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Once. He told me his partner was 'basically family' and that I was making a big deal out of nothing. I felt I'd been called rude for stating a fact.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: That sounds frustrating. And the bills?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: He's two months behind on the electricity. The provider is now threatening to disconnect. He keeps promising he'll pay, but the deadline has passed twice. If they cut the power, I can't study, and I won't be the one who caused it.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Right. Let me make sure I've got this. Noise late into weeknights, a guest staying most of the week without prior agreement, and unpaid bills creating a real risk of disconnection. Is that fair?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Yes, that's all of it. I want to add — Jordan isn't a bad person. He's been kind to me. I just don't think he sees how the things he's doing add up.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: That's an important point, and I'll bring it into the joint meeting. Now, before we meet, I want to ask you something difficult. What do you actually want — to fix this, or to move out?

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: I'd rather fix it. Moving out mid-term is expensive and disruptive. But I need to see real changes, not promises.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Good. In the joint meeting I'll propose three things. A written agreement on quiet hours, a limit on overnight guests, and a fixed plan for the electricity bill — including what happens if he misses another deadline.

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: That sounds reasonable. Should I prepare what I want to say?

body.speaker: RA
body.text: Yes. Write down the facts only — dates, times, amounts. Leave emotions out of the paper, even if they come out in the room. Facts are harder to dismiss than feelings.

body.speaker: Tenant
body.text: Understood. Thank you for taking this seriously. I was honestly expecting to be told to sort it out myself.

body.speaker: RA
body.text: That used to be the standard answer. We've changed how we handle these because too many of them ended in evictions or formal complaints. It's cheaper for everyone if we mediate early. I'll email you both with a time tomorrow morning.

annotations.exampleEn: I should say upfront that I have a conflict of interest.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể sống được, dễ chịu
annotations.exampleEn: I just want the situation to be liveable again.

annotations.meaningVi: làm tôi kiệt sức, làm tôi mòn mỏi
annotations.exampleEn: The constant noise is wearing me down.

annotations.meaningVi: khó tính, căng thẳng quá mức
annotations.exampleEn: He said I was being uptight for asking him to turn the music down.
I assumed it would be common sense, but ours isn't the same.

annotations.meaningVi: đặt ra kỳ vọng (rõ ràng từ đầu)
annotations.exampleEn: It's important to set expectations at the start of a project.

annotations.phrase: making a big deal out of nothing
annotations.pronunciation: /ˈmeɪkɪŋ ə bɪɡ diːl aʊt əv ˈnʌθɪŋ/
annotations.exampleEn: He told me I was making a big deal out of nothing.
He's two months behind on the electricity bill.
The provider has threatened to disconnect us.

annotations.meaningVi: hạn cuối đã trôi qua hai lần
annotations.exampleEn: He keeps promising, but the deadline has passed twice.

annotations.meaningVi: cộng dồn lại (tạo thành vấn đề lớn)
annotations.exampleEn: Small things add up when they happen every day.
Moving out mid-term costs a lot in fees.
We'll sort the rest out in the joint meeting.

annotations.phrase: facts are harder to dismiss than feelings
annotations.meaningVi: sự thật khó bác bỏ hơn cảm xúc
annotations.pronunciation: /fækts ɑː ˈhɑːdə tə dɪsˈmɪs ðən ˈfiːlɪŋz/
annotations.exampleEn: Bring numbers — facts are harder to dismiss than feelings.
I was expecting to be told to sort it out myself.

annotations.meaningVi: kết thúc bằng việc bị đuổi khỏi nhà
annotations.exampleEn: Too many disputes ended in evictions before we started mediating early.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech with 'said' and 'told'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại sử dụng tường thuật gián tiếp để kể lại lời người khác: 'He said I was being uptight', 'He told me his partner was "basically family"', 'He keeps promising he'll pay'. Quy tắc cơ bản: với 'said' không có tân ngữ trực tiếp ('He said + clause'), với 'told' phải có tân ngữ ('He told me + clause'). Thì thường lùi một bậc khi tường thuật (was → is gốc, etc.) nhưng nếu sự thật vẫn còn đúng, có thể giữ nguyên.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses reported speech to relay what others said: 'He said I was being uptight', 'He told me his partner was "basically family"', 'He keeps promising he'll pay'. Basic rule: 'said' takes no direct object ('He said + clause'); 'told' requires an object ('He told me + clause'). Tense usually shifts back one step in reporting (was → is originally), but you may keep present tense if the situation is still true.

grammarNotes.title: First Conditional for Real Consequences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người thuê dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 để mô tả hậu quả thực tế nếu vấn đề không được giải quyết: 'If they cut the power, I can't study, and I won't be the one who caused it', và 'including what happens if he misses another deadline'. Cấu trúc: if + present simple, will/can + verb. Khác với loại 2 (giả định không thực tế), loại 1 dùng cho khả năng thực sự có thể xảy ra.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The tenant uses first conditional to describe real consequences if the problem isn't fixed: 'If they cut the power, I can't study, and I won't be the one who caused it' and 'including what happens if he misses another deadline'. Form: if + present simple, will/can + verb. Unlike second conditional (unreal/hypothetical), first conditional describes situations that can genuinely happen.

translationVi:
Cố vấn ký túc xá: Cảm ơn Marcus đã đến. Tôi đã nói chuyện riêng với Jordan rồi, và tôi muốn nghe phía bạn trước khi cả ba ngồi lại với nhau.

Người thuê: Cảm ơn bạn đã sắp xếp. Tôi muốn nói thẳng từ đầu — tôi không muốn làm Jordan trông xấu. Tôi chỉ muốn tình hình lại sống được.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Đó là cách bắt đầu hay. Bạn có thể kể, bằng lời của mình, chuyện gì đã không ổn trong tháng qua không?

Người thuê: Ba điều thực sự. Tiếng ồn, khách, và hoá đơn. Mỗi cái riêng lẻ tôi có thể chịu được. Cả ba cùng lúc đang làm tôi kiệt sức.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Mình giải quyết từng cái. Kể tôi nghe về tiếng ồn đi.

Người thuê: Jordan bật nhạc đến hai ba giờ sáng hầu hết các đêm trong tuần, và tôi có lớp lúc tám giờ. Tôi đã nhờ vả lịch sự hai lần. Lần đầu cậu ấy giảm nhạc một đêm. Lần thứ hai, cậu ấy nói tôi khó tính. Sau đó tôi không hỏi nữa.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Tôi hiểu sao điều đó làm bạn nản. Lúc dọn vào, hai bạn có thoả thuận giờ yên tĩnh không?

Người thuê: Không, đó là một phần vấn đề. Chúng tôi không đặt ra luật gì. Tôi tưởng đó là lẽ thường, nhưng lẽ thường của chúng tôi không giống nhau.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Rất thường gặp. Đặt kỳ vọng sau khi sự việc đã xảy ra khó hơn đặt từ đầu. Lát mình quay lại chuyện đó. Kể về chuyện khách đi.

Người thuê: Bạn gái cậu ấy ở lại bốn năm đêm mỗi tuần. Tôi chưa bao giờ đồng ý, và căn hộ thì nhỏ. Phòng tắm lúc nào cũng bận, và tôi phải tắm ở phòng tập chỉ để có buổi sáng bình thường.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Bạn đã đề cập với cậu ấy chưa?

Người thuê: Một lần. Cậu ấy bảo bạn gái là 'gần như người nhà' và tôi đang làm to chuyện không đâu. Tôi cảm thấy bị gọi là thô lỗ vì nói ra sự thật.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Nghe bực bội. Còn hoá đơn?

Người thuê: Cậu ấy nợ tiền điện hai tháng. Nhà cung cấp đang doạ cắt. Cậu ấy cứ hứa sẽ trả, nhưng hạn cuối đã trôi qua hai lần. Nếu họ cắt điện, tôi không học được, và tôi sẽ không phải người gây ra chuyện đó.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Được. Để tôi xác nhận. Ồn ào đến khuya các đêm trong tuần, khách ở lại hầu hết tuần không có thoả thuận trước, và hoá đơn chưa trả tạo rủi ro bị cắt điện. Đúng không?

Người thuê: Đúng, tất cả là vậy. Tôi muốn nói thêm — Jordan không phải người xấu. Cậu ấy đã tốt với tôi. Tôi chỉ không nghĩ cậu ấy thấy những việc đang làm cộng dồn lại thế nào.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Điểm quan trọng, tôi sẽ nói trong buổi gặp chung. Trước khi mình họp, tôi muốn hỏi bạn một câu khó. Bạn thực sự muốn gì — sửa chuyện này, hay chuyển ra?

Người thuê: Tôi muốn sửa hơn. Chuyển ra giữa kỳ tốn kém và phiền phức. Nhưng tôi cần thấy thay đổi thực, không phải lời hứa.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Tốt. Trong buổi họp chung tôi sẽ đề xuất ba điều. Một thoả thuận bằng văn bản về giờ yên tĩnh, một giới hạn về khách ở qua đêm, và một kế hoạch cố định cho hoá đơn điện — bao gồm chuyện gì xảy ra nếu cậu ấy lỡ hạn nữa.

Người thuê: Nghe hợp lý. Tôi có nên chuẩn bị điều mình sẽ nói không?

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Có. Viết ra chỉ sự thật — ngày, giờ, số tiền. Để cảm xúc ngoài giấy tờ, dù chúng có thể trào ra trong phòng họp. Sự thật khó bác bỏ hơn cảm xúc.

Người thuê: Hiểu rồi. Cảm ơn bạn đã nghiêm túc. Tôi thật sự đã nghĩ mình sẽ được bảo tự xử lý.

Cố vấn ký túc xá: Đó từng là câu trả lời tiêu chuẩn. Chúng tôi đã thay đổi cách xử lý vì quá nhiều vụ kết thúc bằng việc bị đuổi hoặc khiếu nại chính thức. Hoà giải sớm rẻ hơn cho mọi người. Tôi sẽ gửi email cho cả hai bạn về giờ vào sáng mai.

questions.prompt: Why did the RA already speak to Jordan separately first?
options: To hear each side separately before meeting jointly
questions.explanation: The RA says 'I've already spoken to Jordan separately, and I wanted to hear your side before we sit down with both of you together.'
questions.hint: It's about gathering information before mediating.

questions.prompt: What three issues does Marcus identify?
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'Three things, really. The noise, the guests, and the bills.'

questions.prompt: Until what time does Jordan play music on weeknights?
options: Two or three in the morning
questions.explanation: Marcus says Jordan 'plays music until two or three most weeknights.'
questions.hint: A specific late-night time range is mentioned.

questions.prompt: How did Jordan respond when Marcus asked the second time?
options: He turned the music down for a week
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'The second time, he said I was being uptight.'
questions.hint: Jordan's response shifted from compliance to criticism.

questions.prompt: Did the two roommates set quiet hours when they moved in?
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'No, that's part of the problem. We didn't really set any rules. I assumed it would be common sense.'
questions.hint: Marcus identifies an absence of agreed rules.

questions.prompt: How many nights a week does Jordan's partner stay?
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'His partner stays four or five nights a week.'
Marcus says 'I've started showering at the gym just to get a normal morning.'
questions.hint: Marcus describes a workaround for the bathroom problem.

questions.prompt: How many months is Jordan behind on the electricity bill?
questions.explanation: Marcus says Jordan is 'two months behind on the electricity.'

questions.prompt: What is the real risk of Jordan not paying the bill?
options: The provider will disconnect the power
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'The provider is now threatening to disconnect.'
questions.hint: The threat is from the utility provider.

questions.prompt: Does Marcus want to fix the situation or move out?
options: Fix it, but only if there are real changes, not just promises
Move out only if the RA recommends it
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'I'd rather fix it... But I need to see real changes, not promises.'
questions.hint: Marcus expresses a preference with a condition.

questions.prompt: What three things will the RA propose in the joint meeting?
options: An apology, a fine, and a witness
A written agreement on quiet hours, a limit on overnight guests, and a fixed plan for the electricity bill
Cleaning rotas, kitchen schedules, and a curfew
A move-out date, a refund, and a guarantor
questions.explanation: The RA proposes 'A written agreement on quiet hours, a limit on overnight guests, and a fixed plan for the electricity bill.'
questions.hint: Three specific structural agreements are proposed.

questions.prompt: What does the RA advise Marcus to bring to the meeting?
options: Emotional statements about how he feels
Only the facts — dates, times, amounts — leaving emotions off the paper
questions.explanation: The RA says 'Write down the facts only — dates, times, amounts. Leave emotions out of the paper.'
questions.hint: The advice is about evidence, not feelings on paper.

questions.prompt: Why has the institution changed how it handles these disputes?
options: Because students complained that mediation took too long
Because too many disputes ended in evictions or formal complaints, and early mediation is cheaper for everyone
Because the law now requires it
Because mediators are paid less than lawyers
questions.explanation: The RA says 'too many of them ended in evictions or formal complaints. It's cheaper for everyone if we mediate early.'
questions.hint: The reason combines an outcome problem and a cost argument.

cloze.template:
RA: Thanks for coming in, Marcus. I've already spoken to Jordan separately, and I wanted to hear your side before we sit down together.
Tenant: Thanks for setting this up. I should say {{b1}} — I don't want to make Jordan look bad. I just want the situation to be liveable again.
RA: Could you tell me what's been going wrong?
Tenant: Three things. The noise, the guests, and the bills. Each one alone I could handle. All three at once is wearing me {{b2}}.
RA: Tell me about the noise.
Tenant: Jordan plays music until two or three most weeknights. I've asked twice politely. The second time, he said I was being {{b3}}.
RA: Did you set agreed quiet hours when you moved in?
Tenant: No. I assumed it would be common {{b4}}, but ours isn't the same.
RA: Tell me about the guests.
Tenant: His partner stays four or five nights a week. He told me his partner was 'basically family' and that I was making a big {{b5}} out of nothing.
RA: And the bills?
Tenant: He's two months {{b6}} on the electricity. The provider is threatening to {{b7}}. The deadline has passed twice.
RA: What do you actually want — to fix this, or to move out?
Tenant: I'd rather fix it. Moving out {{b8}} is expensive. But I need to see real changes, not promises.
RA: I'll propose a written agreement on quiet hours, a limit on overnight {{b9}}, and a fixed plan for the electricity bill. Write down the {{b10}} only — leave emotions out of the paper.

blanks.explanation: 'Upfront' specifically means 'honestly and at the very start' — the exact word the tenant uses.
'Wear someone down' is the fixed phrasal verb meaning to exhaust by repeated stress.
'Uptight' is the exact word Jordan used — meaning overly tense or strict.
'Common sense' is the fixed collocation — practical judgement shared by reasonable people.
'Making a big deal out of nothing' is the fixed idiom — the exact phrase used.
'Behind on the electricity' is the standard expression for unpaid bills accumulating.
'Disconnect' is the technical verb utilities use — the exact word the tenant uses.
'Mid-term' specifically means in the middle of a contract or term — fits the housing context.
'Overnight guests' is the fixed phrase used in housing agreements.
The RA says 'Write down the facts only' — facts as opposed to emotions.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The RA tells Marcus that 'facts are harder to dismiss than feelings'. Is that always true in a personal dispute, or are there situations where someone's feelings should weigh more heavily than the verifiable facts?
